Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 2, new layout with multiple colored diamonds/objects connected by paths - Purple ring (empty center) on right side, white diamond (orange center), gray diamonds (orange centers), orange object (magenta center), purple square (magenta center), orange diamond (bottom) - Pattern from Level 1: click ring's empty center first, then click object centers in order along the path
- Plan: Click the new p centers
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 46 actions; a strong score needs about 33 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content: New state 33! Let me check what changed after clicking (20, 47).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
after = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("=== Changed cells ===")
for r in range(len(after)):
    for c in range(len(after[r])):
        if r < len(before) and c < len(before[r]) and after[r][c] != before[r][c]:
            print(f"  ({r},{c}): {before[r][c]} -> {after[r][c]}")

# Check key colored centers
ascii_grid = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Key colored centers ===")
for r in range(len(ascii_grid)):
    for c in range(len(ascii_grid[r])):
        ch = ascii_grid[r][c]
        if ch in 'MO':
            print(f"  ({r},{c}): {ch}")

print("\n=== White pixels ===")
for r in range(len(ascii_grid)):
    for c in range(len(ascii_grid[r])):
        if ascii_grid[r][c] == 'w':
            print(f"  ({r},{c}): w")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking (20, 47) toggled:
- White pixels at (10-14, 49-53) turned to gray (the first gray diamond)
- Gray pixels at (18-22, 45-49) turned to white (the second gray diamond)
So clicking (20, 47) toggles the second gray diamond. The pattern is:
- Clicking (12, 51) toggles gray diamond 1 (10-14, 49-53)
- Clicking (20, 47) toggles gray diamond 2 (18-22, 45-49)
- Clicking (16, 49) toggles white pixels between two positions
Now I have:
- White pixels at (18-22, 45-49)
- Gray pixels at (10-14, 49-53) and (25-28, 45-48)
- M at (16, 49) - orange object
- M at (29, 44) - purple square
- O at (12, 51) - gray diamond (now gray)
- O at (20, 47) - gray diamond (now white)
Looking at the image, I can see the white pixels are now in the position of the second gray diamond. The purple square is at (29, 44).
Let me think about what the goal might be. Looking at the pattern:
1. Purple ring center click - moves purple square
2. Purple square M clicks - move purple square along path
3. Orange object M click - toggles white pixels
4. Gray diamond O clicks - toggle gray diamonds to white
The goal might be to turn all gray diamonds/objects to white, or to move the purple square to a specific position.
Looking at the image, I see the purple square is at (29, 44) and there's an orange diamond at the bottom. Maybe I need to move the purple square to the orange diamond position.
But the purple square can't move further from (29, 44) - clicking it was no_impact.
Let me try clicking the orange diamond center. The orange diamond is at (48-54, 37-43). The center would be around (51, 40).
Actually, looking at the orange diamond more carefully:
```
Row 48: BBBOBOBB
Row 49: BBOBBBOB
Row 50: BOBBBBBO
Row 51: BBBBBBBB
Row 52: BOBBBBBO
Row 53: BBOBBBOB
Row 54: BBBOBOBB
```
The center is at (51, 40) which is B. But wait, looking at the pattern, the orange diamond has O on the border and B in the interior. The "center" might be the empty space in the middle.
Actually, looking more carefully, the orange diamond is a hollow diamond shape made of O pixels with B interior. The center would be at (51, 40) which is B.
Let me try clicking (51, 40) to see if it does something.
